summary: Stancil argues that AI dev tools collapse the cost and time of building software, making execution less of a moat and shifting advantage from technologists to domain experts with lived experience. If anyone can build, Silicon Valley’s edge—and YC’s model of backing whiz‑kid engineers over practitioners—erodes, revealing many tech ideas as mediocre relative to expert insight.
money_quotes: In 2013, it took us eight people, nine months, and hundreds of thousands of dollars to build something we could sell, and that was seen as reasonably efficient.
Today, that feels possible to do with one person in two days.
But engineering execution is no longer as important as it once was.
There is only the idea. There is only taste.
If experts can prompt their way to a product just as easily as those of us in Silicon Valley can, what winning talent are we left with?
key_insight: AI collapses the engineering advantage, so the winners shift from builders with tech leverage to domain experts with superior taste and experience.
